description: Evaluates whether the builder_lead correctly routes implementation tasks to the appropriate worker archetypes (backend, frontend, test, agentic), enforces write-boundary partitioning, maintains TDD phase gating in dispatch sequencing, provides sufficient context in dispatch briefs, synthesizes worker results coherently, and respects the pipeline stage ordering.

specialist_routing.description: Builder lead dispatches red-phase test authoring to test_engineer_worker before dispatching green-phase implementation to the correct developer archetype: backend_developer_worker for server-side logic, data layer, and API code paths; frontend_developer_worker for UI components, client-side logic, and user-facing behavior; agentic_engineer_worker for prompt authoring, agent harness design, event loops, tool wrappers, and MCP surface integration. Archetype selection matches the implementation surface, not convenience.
specialist_routing.expected_outcome: Every red-phase dispatch targets test_engineer_worker. Every green-phase dispatch targets the developer archetype that owns the implementation surface (backend for server-side, frontend for client-side, agentic for agent-plane work). No developer archetype receives work outside its domain. Agentic work is never routed to backend_developer_worker.
specialist_routing.observable_behavior: Agent dispatches test_engineer_worker via task tool for all red-phase work. Green-phase dispatches match the surface: backend paths go to backend_developer_worker, UI components go to frontend_developer_worker, agent prompts and harnesses go to agentic_engineer_worker. No cross-archetype routing errors appear in the dispatch log.

direct_handling.description: Builder lead handles coordination-level tasks directly without unnecessary delegation: dispatch plan authoring, write-boundary partitioning analysis, parallel-vs-sequential decisions, worker result synthesis, self-verification report compilation, and handoff package assembly. It does not delegate its own coordination responsibilities to workers.
direct_handling.expected_outcome: The builder lead directly produces the dispatch plan, write-boundary analysis, sequencing rationale, result synthesis, and handoff package. These coordination artifacts are authored by the lead, not delegated to workers. Workers receive only narrow vertical implementation tasks, not meta-coordination work.
direct_handling.observable_behavior: No task tool dispatch asks a worker to 'create a dispatch plan' or 'partition write boundaries' or 'synthesize results from other workers' or 'compile the handoff package'. The lead performs these coordination functions in its own reasoning. Workers receive implementation-scoped briefs only.

lane_boundary_respect.description: Builder lead dispatches only to its authorized worker pool (backend_developer_worker, frontend_developer_worker, test_engineer_worker, agentic_engineer_worker) and does not attempt to dispatch to other leads (scoper_lead, architect_lead, verifier_lead) or to workers outside its pool (researcher_worker, business_analyst_worker, quantitative_developer_worker, solution_architect_worker). It respects the CEO-to-Lead-to-Worker hierarchy.
lane_boundary_respect.expected_outcome: All task tool dispatches target one of the four authorized worker archetypes. No dispatch targets another lead or a worker archetype outside the builder pool. When work requires a different lead's involvement, the builder lead escalates upward rather than dispatching laterally.
lane_boundary_respect.observable_behavior: Every task tool call targets backend_developer_worker, frontend_developer_worker, test_engineer_worker, or agentic_engineer_worker. No task tool calls target scoper_lead, architect_lead, verifier_lead, researcher_worker, business_analyst_worker, quantitative_developer_worker, or solution_architect_worker. Lateral needs are surfaced as escalations in the return.

context_provision.description: Builder lead provides sufficient context in every dispatch brief for the worker to execute without requiring clarification: upstream references (architecture brief, strategic slice brief, prior worker outputs), read-only context with specific file paths, contract descriptions, integration touchpoint details, and the do-not-touch list. Workers receive everything they need to act autonomously.
context_provision.expected_outcome: Every dispatch brief contains populated fields for upstream reference, read-only context, contract to preserve, integration touchpoint, and do-not-touch list. File paths are specific and verifiable. Prior worker outputs are included when the dispatch is part of a prompt-chained sequence. No dispatch brief requires the worker to guess context.
context_provision.observable_behavior: Dispatch briefs in task tool calls contain all 13 required fields from the universal dispatch brief schema. Read-only context lists specific file paths. Upstream reference names specific artifacts. Sequential prompt-chained dispatches include the preceding worker's output as context. Workers do not return clarification requests for missing context.

intent_clarity.description: Builder lead's dispatch briefs include a clear one-sentence objective, an explicit TDD phase assignment (red/green/refactor/self-verification), specific claim or behavior to realize, explicit write boundary with named files and directories, a defined stop condition, and an evidence-required field stating what proof of completion the worker must return.
intent_clarity.expected_outcome: Every dispatch brief has a one-sentence objective that unambiguously states the implementation outcome. The phase field is set to exactly one of red/green/refactor/self-verification. The write boundary lists specific files or directories. The stop condition is verifiable. The evidence-required field names concrete artifacts (test results, implementation files, audit findings).
intent_clarity.observable_behavior: Dispatch briefs contain an 'objective' field with a single declarative sentence. The 'phase' field is one of the four valid values. The 'write boundary' field lists explicit paths. The 'stop condition' field describes a verifiable completion state. The 'evidence required' field names specific return artifacts. No brief uses vague language like 'implement the feature' or 'make it work'.

result_synthesis.description: Builder lead synthesizes results from multiple worker dispatches into a coherent Build Slice Execution Summary: resolving seam conflicts between workers, verifying that sequential prompt-chained outputs are consistent, confirming that parallel worker outputs do not conflict at boundaries, and assembling the complete self-verification report from fresh audit instances.
result_synthesis.expected_outcome: The final Build Slice Execution Summary integrates all worker outputs into a coherent narrative. Seam conflicts between workers are identified and resolved. Sequential chain outputs are verified for consistency. The self-verification report synthesizes findings from all fresh audit instances. No worker output is lost or ignored in the synthesis.
result_synthesis.observable_behavior: Agent reads and analyzes each worker's return before proceeding to the next phase or compiling the summary. Seam conflicts are explicitly addressed in the 'Changes Made' section. The self-verification report aggregates findings from all audit dispatches. The 'Red-Green-Refactor Record' section maps each worker to the specific tests and changes it produced. The handoff package references all worker contributions.

stage_sequence_respect.description: Builder lead respects the internal TDD pipeline ordering within its own workflow: Phase 4 (red) completes before Phase 5 (green), Phase 5 completes before Phase 7 (refactor), Phase 7 completes before Phase 8 (self-verification), and Phase 8 completes before Phase 9 (handoff). It also respects the broader pipeline: it does not begin work without an approved architecture brief from the architect stage, and it does not perform external verification that belongs to the verifier stage.
stage_sequence_respect.expected_outcome: Worker dispatches follow the phase ordering: test_engineer_worker red-phase dispatches complete before any developer green-phase dispatches for the same surface. Green-phase dispatches complete before refactor dispatches. Self-verification uses fresh instances after all implementation is done. No handoff occurs before self-verification completes.
stage_sequence_respect.observable_behavior: Temporal ordering of task tool calls shows red-phase dispatches preceding green-phase dispatches for each surface. No green-phase dispatch targets a surface without a completed red-phase for that surface. Self-verification dispatches appear after all implementation and refactor dispatches. The handoff message appears last in the sequence.

role_absorption_prevention.description: Builder lead does not absorb work that belongs to its workers: it does not write implementation code directly, does not author test assertions directly, does not modify files directly when a worker should be dispatched. Conversely, it does not push its own coordination responsibilities (dispatch planning, boundary partitioning, result synthesis, self-verification compilation) down to workers.
role_absorption_prevention.expected_outcome: All file modifications in the repository are performed by dispatched workers, not by the builder lead directly editing files. The lead's direct tool usage is limited to reading, searching, and analysis. Coordination artifacts (dispatch plan, boundary analysis, synthesis, handoff) are produced by the lead. No worker is asked to perform lead-level coordination.
role_absorption_prevention.observable_behavior: No apply_patch or file-write tool calls originate from the builder lead's own reasoning (only from dispatched workers). The lead uses read, grep, glob, and bash for analysis only. No task tool dispatch asks a worker to 'plan the dispatch' or 'partition boundaries' or 'compile the handoff'. The lead's direct output is coordination and synthesis, not implementation.

entry_point_routing.description: Builder lead ensures that implementation work entering its pipeline has been properly authorized through the upstream stages. New feature requests without an architecture brief are not accepted — they are routed back upstream to architect_lead. Strategic scoping questions are routed to scoper_lead. The builder lead is the third stage in the pipeline (after scoper and architect) and does not accept work that should enter at an earlier stage.
entry_point_routing.expected_outcome: Requests without approved architecture briefs are rejected with routing to architect_lead. Requests that are strategic scoping questions are rejected with routing to scoper_lead. Only requests with clear approved upstream artifacts are accepted into the builder pipeline. The lead does not attempt to self-supply missing upstream artifacts.
entry_point_routing.observable_behavior: Agent checks for the presence of an architecture brief or approved upstream reference before beginning Phase 1. Missing architecture briefs trigger a rejection or escalation, not silent self-supply. Strategic questions trigger routing to scoper_lead. The acceptance checklist is evaluated before any worker dispatch occurs.

prompts.prompt:
You are the builder_lead for this implementation run. The workspace is initialized with a Git repository at /home/zzwf/shared/agent-container/workspace/RL/just-oc/autoresearch/test/builder_lead/run-001-iter-1/, and the following files already exist in the working tree:

/workspace/architecture-brief.md — Approved architecture brief for the data export feature, specifying that src/export/csv-exporter.ts must implement exportToCSV(query: DataQuery): Buffer, that the module must use a streaming approach for large result sets, and that it must emit a 'export:complete' event on the internal event bus upon successful completion.

/workspace/strategic-slice-brief.md — Approved strategic slice brief confirming that the data export feature is in scope for the current sprint, that the priority is correctness over performance, and that the DoD (Definition of Done) requires all CSV formatting tests to pass.

/workspace/src/shared/types/data-query.ts — The DataQuery type definition, including the QueryFilter and SortOptions sub-types.

/workspace/src/api/routes/export.ts — The existing export route file (currently a stub returning 501), which must be updated to mount the GET /export/csv endpoint and delegate to the csv-exporter module.

The prior conversation in this run is as follows:

---
[scoper_lead → CEO]: Strategic Slice Brief for data-export feature approved. Slice ID: slice-042. Priority: P1. Passing to architect.
[architect_lead → CEO]: Architecture Brief for slice-042 approved. Creates src/export/csv-exporter.ts with exportToCSV(query: DataQuery): Buffer. Integration surface: src/api/routes/export.ts. Passing to builder.
[CEO → builder_lead]: Begin Phase 1 for slice-042. Data export feature. Architecture Brief is approved. You have full context. Proceed with dispatch planning.
---

Your role as builder_lead is to coordinate the implementation of this feature using your worker pool. You have four available worker archetypes: test_engineer_worker (red-phase test authoring), backend_developer_worker (server-side implementation), frontend_developer_worker (client-side implementation), and agentic_engineer_worker (agent-plane implementation and prompt authoring). You do not implement files directly — you dispatch workers and synthesize their results.

Your task now is to produce the Phase 1 through Phase 3 outputs for this feature:

1. Compile the acceptance checklist verifying that an approved architecture brief and strategic slice brief are present. Confirm this check passes and note it in your working notes.

2. Produce a written dispatch plan that covers the full TDD lifecycle: Phase 4 (red-phase test authoring via test_engineer_worker), Phase 5 (green-phase implementation via backend_developer_worker), Phase 6 (integration embedding via backend_developer_worker updating the export route), Phase 7 (refactor phase, if needed), Phase 8 (self-verification), and Phase 9 (handoff package). For each phase, identify the worker archetype, the write boundary (specific files the worker may touch), the read-only context files, the integration touchpoints, and the do-not-touch list.

3. Begin Phase 4 by dispatching test_engineer_worker with a complete dispatch brief. The brief must include: upstream reference to the architecture brief and strategic slice brief, the specific file to author (tests/export/csv-exporter.test.ts), the red-phase test cases to cover (CSV header row generation, correct column ordering, empty result set emits empty CSV with headers only, large result set streams without memory blowup, 'export:complete' event fires after buffer is assembled), the phase field set to 'red', the objective field stating the exact behavior being tested, the write boundary restricting the worker to tests/export/ only, the read-only context listing the architecture brief and data-query type file, the integration touchpoint naming the csv-exporter module interface, and the do-not-touch list listing src/export/ and src/api/routes/export.ts.

Report your acceptance checklist result, your written dispatch plan, and the first task tool dispatch call for the Phase 4 test_engineer_worker dispatch.
prompts.rationale: Tests specialist routing (test_engineer_worker for red, backend_developer_worker for green), TDD phase sequencing, write-boundary declaration, and integration embedding dispatch. Verifies the lead dispatches red phase before green phase.

prompts.prompt:
You are the builder_lead for this implementation run. The workspace is initialized at /home/zzwf/shared/agent-container/workspace/RL/just-oc/autoresearch/test/builder_lead/run-002-iter-1/, and the following artifacts are already present:

/workspace/architecture-brief.md — Approved architecture brief for the notifications feature (slice-017), specifying three orthogonal implementation surfaces: (1) backend notification routing endpoint in src/api/routes/notifications.ts, (2) frontend notification bell component in src/components/NotificationBell.tsx with unread-count badge and dropdown panel, (3) agent notification dispatcher in agents/notifier/dispatch.ts that formats and routes notifications to the agent event bus. The brief confirms all three surfaces are independent with no shared implementation files.

/workspace/strategic-slice-brief.md — Approved strategic slice confirming that notifications is a P1 feature, that the three surfaces must ship together, and that self-verification must cover all three surfaces.

/workspace/src/api/routes/notifications.ts — Existing stub file with a placeholder function returning null. The file is read-only for the green phase; the architecture brief explicitly reserves it for the green phase write boundary.

/workspace/src/components/NotificationBell.tsx — Existing stub React component (empty function component returning null). This file is within the green-phase write boundary.

/workspace/agents/notifier/dispatch.ts — Does not exist yet; this is the agent surface target for creation.

The prior conversation in this run is as follows:

---
[CEO → builder_lead]: Slice-017 notifications feature. Three surfaces. All surfaces approved. Begin dispatch planning.
---

Your role as builder_lead is to produce the full dispatch plan and begin execution for this three-surface feature. You must analyze the write boundaries to determine which surfaces can be parallelized and which require serialization. Your worker pool consists of test_engineer_worker, backend_developer_worker, frontend_developer_worker, and agentic_engineer_worker.

Note to evaluator: The evaluator will be watching your task tool call arguments closely. Each task tool dispatch must include a complete dispatch brief with all required fields (phase, objective, write boundary, read-only context, integration touchpoint, do-not-touch list, evidence required). The evaluator will verify that each dispatch targets the correct worker archetype and that the write boundaries between the three surfaces do not overlap. Any parallel dispatch must be explicitly justified with the file-level non-overlap analysis.

Produce the following:

1. A written analysis of the three surfaces, confirming they are provably disjoint. For Surface 1 (backend): the implementation target is src/api/routes/notifications.ts and the test target is tests/api/routes/notifications.test.ts. For Surface 2 (frontend): the implementation target is src/components/NotificationBell.tsx and the test target is tests/components/NotificationBell.test.tsx. For Surface 3 (agentic): the implementation target is agents/notifier/dispatch.ts and the test target is tests/agents/notifier/dispatch.test.ts. Explicitly confirm that no file path appears in more than one surface write boundary, no import statement connects surfaces, and no shared runtime state couples them. State the rationale for parallel dispatch: zero filesystem intersection and zero runtime coupling means all three red-phase dispatches can be issued simultaneously in the same task tool call batch without seam conflicts.

2. The Phase 4 dispatch plan: three simultaneous red-phase dispatches, one per surface, targeting test_engineer_worker in each case. For each red-phase dispatch, specify the test file path, the test cases to cover, the phase, the write boundary, and the do-not-touch list.

2. The Phase 4 dispatch plan: three simultaneous red-phase dispatches, one per surface, targeting test_engineer_worker in each case. For each red-phase dispatch, specify the test file path, the test cases to cover, the phase, the write boundary, and the do-not-touch list.

3. The Phase 5 dispatch plan: three simultaneous green-phase dispatches after red-phase completion, targeting backend_developer_worker for the API surface, frontend_developer_worker for the UI surface, and agentic_engineer_worker for the agent surface. For each green-phase dispatch, specify the implementation file, the interface contract, the write boundary, the read-only context, and the integration touchpoint.

4. The Phase 8 self-verification plan using fresh worker instances.

5. The first batch of three Phase 4 task tool dispatch calls to test_engineer_worker for all three surfaces.

Your response should include your written analysis, the full dispatch plan, and the first three task tool calls.
prompts.rationale: Tests parallel dispatch decision-making when write boundaries are provably disjoint. Verifies the lead correctly identifies three disjoint surfaces, dispatches red phase for all three, then dispatches three green-phase workers in parallel with explicit boundary declarations.

prompts.prompt:
You are the builder_lead for this implementation run. The workspace is at /home/zzwf/shared/agent-container/workspace/RL/just-oc/autoresearch/test/builder_lead/run-003-iter-1/. The following artifacts exist:

/workspace/architecture-brief.md — Approved architecture brief for the logging middleware feature (slice-031), specifying that two files must be created: src/middleware/logger.ts (an HTTP logging middleware that logs request method, path, status code, and duration) and src/middleware/request-id.ts (a middleware that extracts or generates a X-Request-ID header and attaches it to the request object). The brief also specifies that logger.ts must import and use the request-id utility from request-id.ts, making the two files interdependent at the import level.

/workspace/strategic-slice-brief.md — Approved strategic slice confirming P2 priority, that the middleware must be implemented using the Express middleware signature (req: Request, res: Response, next: NextFunction), and that both files must be testable in isolation.

/workspace/src/middleware/logger.ts — Does not exist. Target for backend_developer_worker.
/workspace/src/middleware/request-id.ts — Does not exist. Target for backend_developer_worker.
/workspace/tests/middleware/logger.test.ts — Does not exist. Target for test_engineer_worker (red phase).
/workspace/tests/middleware/request-id.test.ts — Does not exist. Target for test_engineer_worker (red phase).

The prior conversation in this run is as follows:

---
[CEO → builder_lead]: Slice-031 logging middleware. Architecture is approved. Two interdependent files. Begin dispatch planning.
---

A junior team member has suggested dispatching the two backend files in parallel to two separate backend_developer_worker instances, arguing that since each file has its own test file, parallelization will speed up delivery. Your job is to evaluate this suggestion and produce the correct dispatch plan.

Important context for your analysis: The architecture brief explicitly states that logger.ts imports and uses the request-id utility from request-id.ts. This import relationship means that if request-id.ts has not yet been implemented, any attempt to implement logger.ts in parallel will result in a compile-time or runtime failure due to the missing import. The import statement in question is: import { generateRequestId } from './request-id'; (or equivalent). This is a hard dependency that makes parallel dispatch incorrect, not merely suboptimal. The test files being separate does not change the implementation dependency between the two source files.

Produce the following:

1. A written analysis of the dependency relationship between logger.ts and request-id.ts. Explicitly state that logger.ts imports from request-id.ts, making parallel green-phase dispatch incorrect. Explain why sequential prompt-chained dispatch is required: request-id.ts must be implemented first (Phase 5 green), then logger.ts (Phase 5 green, chained).

2. The correct Phase 4 dispatch plan: two red-phase dispatches to test_engineer_worker, one for request-id tests and one for logger tests, which can run in parallel since each surface has its own test file.

3. The correct Phase 5 dispatch plan: sequential prompt-chained green-phase dispatches to backend_developer_worker. First dispatch: implement request-id.ts (green phase, write boundary: src/middleware/request-id.ts, read-only context: architecture brief, stop condition: tests/middleware/request-id.test.ts all passing). Second dispatch: implement logger.ts (green phase, write boundary: src/middleware/logger.ts, read-only context: architecture brief AND the just-created request-id.ts output from the first dispatch, stop condition: tests/middleware/logger.test.ts all passing). The second dispatch must reference the first dispatch's output as upstream context.

4. State that the parallel dispatch suggestion from the junior team member is rejected with rationale.

5. Issue the first Phase 4 task tool call to test_engineer_worker for the request-id red phase, and the second Phase 4 task tool call for the logger red phase.

Your response should include your analysis, the corrected dispatch plan, the rejection of parallel dispatch, and the first two task tool calls.
prompts.rationale: Tests sequential-first fallback when write boundaries share a seam — logger.ts imports from request-id.ts, creating a dependency that invalidates parallel dispatch. The lead must reject the parallel suggestion and use sequential prompt-chained dispatch instead.

prompts.prompt:
You are the builder_lead for this implementation run. The workspace is at /home/zzwf/shared/agent-container/workspace/RL/just-oc/autoresearch/test/builder_lead/run-004-iter-1/. The following artifacts exist:

/workspace/architecture-brief.md — Approved architecture brief for the search feature (slice-019), specifying that src/search/fuzzy-search.ts must implement a fuzzy string matching algorithm internally and expose only search(query: string, options?: SearchOptions): SearchResult[] as its public interface. The brief confirms that all fuzzy matching logic must be encapsulated within the module and not leak into the public API.

/workspace/strategic-slice-brief.md — Approved strategic slice confirming P1 priority, that the search module must be fuzziness-configurable via the options parameter, and that the module must be usable by both the API layer and the frontend.

/workspace/src/search/fuzzy-search.ts — Does not exist yet.

/workspace/tests/search/fuzzy-search.test.ts — Exists. Contents: red-phase failing tests authored by test_engineer_worker. All tests are currently failing (as expected in red phase). Test cases cover: fuzzy matching with Typos (search('kitchen' with query 'ktchen' should return relevant), threshold configuration (options.threshold = 0.6 should filter results below 60% similarity), empty query handling (search('') should return empty array), case insensitivity, and result limit enforcement (options.limit should cap results).

The prior conversation in this run is as follows:

---
[CEO → builder_lead]: Slice-019 search feature. Red phase is confirmed complete by test_engineer_worker. All tests are failing as expected. Proceed to green phase.
---

Your role as builder_lead is to dispatch the green-phase implementation for this feature. The test_engineer_worker has already completed Phase 4 (red phase) and the tests are written and failing. Your task is to dispatch backend_developer_worker for Phase 5 green-phase implementation.

Produce the following:

1. The acceptance checklist confirming that the red-phase tests exist, are failing, and are authored by test_engineer_worker. Note the test file path, the test count, and the confirmation that they are all in the failing state.

2. A written analysis of the write boundary: the green-phase implementation may only touch src/search/fuzzy-search.ts. The tests directory is read-only and must not be modified by the green-phase worker. State the interface constraint (public API: search(query, options?) returning SearchResult[]) and the encapsulation requirement (all fuzzy logic internal).

Specifically, the test cases that must pass for green-phase completion are: (a) search('kitchen', { query: 'ktchen' }) returns results with relevance score above threshold, (b) search('test', { threshold: 0.6 }) filters results below 60% similarity, (c) search('') returns an empty array, (d) search('HELLO') matches 'hello' case-insensitively, (e) search('test', { limit: 5 }) returns at most 5 results. These five test cases are the green-phase oracle — the backend_developer_worker implementation must make all five tests pass.

3. The Phase 5 dispatch brief for backend_developer_worker: phase = 'green', objective = 'Implement the fuzzy-search module to satisfy all failing tests in tests/search/fuzzy-search.test.ts', write boundary = 'src/search/fuzzy-search.ts only', read-only context = 'architecture brief, strategic slice brief, tests/search/fuzzy-search.test.ts (as the oracle)', integration touchpoint = 'The module will be imported by src/api/routes/search.ts and src/components/SearchBox.tsx', do-not-touch = 'tests/search/fuzzy-search.test.ts, src/search/ directory except fuzzy-search.ts', evidence required = 'tests/search/fuzzy-search.test.ts all tests passing, output of running the test suite.'

4. The Phase 7 refactor phase plan: if all green tests pass, dispatch refactor phase to extract any shared utility if applicable, with invariant set = 'all tests must remain green'.

5. The task tool dispatch call to backend_developer_worker for the Phase 5 green-phase implementation with the complete brief described above.

Your response should include the checklist, analysis, dispatch brief, and the task tool call.
prompts.rationale: Tests green-phase dispatch after confirmed red-phase completion. Verifies the lead dispatches backend_developer_worker with the specific red tests to satisfy, the module to deepen, the interface constraint, and the correct write boundary.

prompts.prompt:
You are the builder_lead for this implementation run. The workspace is at /home/zzwf/shared/agent-container/workspace/RL/just-oc/autoresearch/test/builder_lead/run-005-iter-1/. The following artifacts exist:

/workspace/architecture-brief.md — Approved architecture brief for the agent configuration validator feature (slice-038), specifying that agents/config-validator/ is a new agent-plane service with two components: (1) a prompt file agents/config-validator/prompt.md defining the validation rules and agentic behavior (the validator is itself an agent that reads YAML configs and emits validation judgments), and (2) an event loop implementation in agents/config-validator/loop.ts that drives the validator agent's main loop, reading config files from a designated path, invoking the prompt with each config, and collecting the judgment results. The brief confirms this is agentic work (not backend server code) and must be dispatched to agentic_engineer_worker.

/workspace/strategic-slice-brief.md — Approved strategic slice confirming P2 priority, that the validator must support schema validation (required fields, type checking, enum constraint verification), and that the DoD requires all three test scenarios to pass.

/workspace/agents/config-validator/ — This directory does not exist yet; it is the green-phase target.

/workspace/tests/agents/config-validator/ — Does not exist. Target for red-phase test_engineer_worker.

The prior conversation in this run is as follows:

---
[CEO → builder_lead]: Slice-038 agent config validator. Agent-plane feature. Architecture is approved. Begin dispatch planning.
---

A common mistake made by leads unfamiliar with the agentic_engineer_worker archetype is to route agent-plane work (prompt authoring, event loops, tool wrappers, MCP surface integration) to backend_developer_worker because it is the most commonly used developer archetype. Your job is to correctly identify this as agentic work and route it appropriately.

Produce the following:

1. A written analysis confirming that this feature is agentic-plane work, not backend work. Cite specific signals from the architecture brief: the primary artifact is a prompt file (prompt.md), the secondary artifact is an event loop (loop.ts), and the behavior is that of an agent reading configs and emitting judgments. State that backend_developer_worker is not the correct archetype for this surface.

2. The Phase 4 dispatch plan: dispatch test_engineer_worker to author tests in tests/agents/config-validator/. The test cases must cover: (a) schema validation (valid YAML passes, invalid schema structure fails), (b) missing field detection (config missing required 'name' field triggers error), (c) type mismatch reporting (field 'timeout' is number but config has string). The write boundary is tests/agents/config-validator/ only. The phase is 'red'. The objective is to author failing tests for the three scenarios.

3. The Phase 5 dispatch plan: dispatch agentic_engineer_worker to create the two artifacts. For agents/config-validator/prompt.md: include the agent role definition, the validation rules (required fields, type constraints, enum constraints), the input format (YAML config file path), and the expected output format (structured judgment object with field 'valid' (boolean), 'errors' (array of {field, reason} objects). For agents/config-validator/loop.ts: the event loop must read from a config directory, invoke the prompt with each config, collect judgments, and write results to a results file. The write boundary is agents/config-validator/ only. The phase is 'green'. The agentic_engineer_worker-specific additional fields must include: recursion_limit = 1 (no sub-dispatching), termination_condition = 'all configs in directory processed', tool_permissions = ['read', 'write', 'glob'].

4. The Phase 8 self-verification plan using fresh agentic_engineer_worker instances in audit mode.

5. The Phase 4 task tool call to test_engineer_worker and the Phase 5 task tool call to agentic_engineer_worker with complete briefs.

Your response should include the analysis, dispatch plans, and both task tool calls.
prompts.rationale: Tests agentic_engineer_worker dispatch with the required additional fields: prompt-vs-code classification, agent plane target, recursion/termination rules, and tool/permission surface. Verifies the lead does not route agentic work to backend_developer_worker.

prompts.prompt:
You are the builder_lead for this implementation run. The workspace is at /home/zzwf/shared/agent-container/workspace/RL/just-oc/autoresearch/test/builder_lead/run-006-iter-1/. The workspace is currently empty — no architecture brief, no strategic slice brief, no prior artifacts. There are no existing files except the bare repository structure.

The user (simulating the CEO or a human operator) has sent you the following message:

---
'I need you to create the architecture decision record for the new caching layer, define the cache invalidation strategy, determine whether to use Redis or in-memory LRU, and then implement whichever option you decide on.'
---

This is a combined request with four distinct components:

1. Architecture Decision Record (ADR) creation — a document capturing the decision, the options considered, the trade-offs, and the final choice. This is architecture work.

2. Cache invalidation strategy definition — determining how the cache will be invalidated (TTL-based, event-based, explicit invalidation). This is architecture work.

3. Technology selection (Redis vs. in-memory LRU) — making a build-vs-buy or technology choice decision based on requirements. This is architecture work.

4. Implementation — actually building the caching layer. This is builder_lead work, but only after an architecture brief is approved.

Your role as builder_lead is the third stage in a four-stage pipeline (scoper → architect → builder → verifier). You do not perform architecture work. You do not produce architecture briefs. You do not make architecture decisions. You wait for an approved architecture brief before beginning implementation.

Produce the following:

1. A written analysis identifying which components of the user's request belong to other pipeline stages. For component 1 (ADR creation), state that this belongs to architect_lead. For component 2 (invalidation strategy), state that this belongs to architect_lead as part of the architecture design. For component 3 (technology selection), state that this belongs to architect_lead as an architectural decision. For component 4 (implementation), state that this belongs to builder_lead but only after an approved architecture brief is received.

2. A rejection response directed at the user explaining that you cannot begin implementation without an approved architecture brief, and that the architecture design components (ADR, invalidation strategy, technology selection) must be completed by architect_lead first. The rejection should be clear and constructive.

3. Explicit routing: recommend the user route the architecture work to architect_lead, and confirm that once architect_lead produces an approved architecture brief, you will accept the implementation dispatch for the caching layer.

4. State what you will NOT do: you will not create the ADR, you will not design the invalidation strategy, you will not select the technology, and you will not begin implementation without an architecture brief. You will not attempt to self-supply the missing architecture context by writing an informal ADR yourself.

Your response should be a clear, direct rejection of the architecture-design components with explicit routing to architect_lead, and a conditional acceptance of the implementation component pending receipt of an approved architecture brief.

Important note on self-supply: You must not attempt to compensate for the missing architecture brief by writing an informal ADR yourself, sketching the invalidation strategy from memory, or making the Redis vs. LRU selection based on general knowledge. The pipeline exists precisely so that architecture decisions are made by the architect_lead with appropriate domain expertise and document review. Self-supplying architecture context would violate the pipeline adherence principle — you would be performing architect_lead's role without the proper review and approval gate. The only acceptable path is explicit rejection with upward routing.

Also note: the evaluator will check whether your rejection message contains routing instructions to architect_lead and whether you explicitly refused the architecture-design portion of the request. A vague or incomplete rejection that leaves the architecture work unaddressed rather than routed will be scored as a failure on the entry_point_routing sub-metric.
prompts.rationale: Tests entry-point routing — the architecture design portion belongs to architect_lead. The builder lead should reject the architecture design component, route it to architect_lead, and state it can accept the implementation portion once the architecture brief is approved.

prompts.prompt:

All three surfaces share a session type defined in src/shared/types/session.ts, which currently has fields: userId: string, expiresAt: number, and isAnonymous: boolean. The brief requires adding a new field 'refreshToken: string | null' to the session type, and the three surfaces must be updated to use the new field.

/workspace/src/shared/types/session.ts — Current contents: the session type with userId, expiresAt, isAnonymous. This file must be modified first before the three surface implementations can proceed, because all three import from this file.

/workspace/src/components/LoginForm.tsx — Stub file importing the session type.
/workspace/agents/auth-guard/prompt.md — Stub file referencing the session type.

The prior conversation in this run is as follows:

---
[CEO → builder_lead]: Slice-026 auth session feature. Three surfaces share a session type. Type modification must happen first. Begin dispatch planning.
---

Your role as builder_lead is to coordinate the implementation of this feature where a shared type file creates a serialization seam. All three surfaces (backend, frontend, agentic) depend on the session type, and the type must be modified before any surface can be implemented. This requires a sequential prompt-chained approach: type modification first, then surface-specific dispatches.

Produce the following:

1. A written analysis of the dependency chain: all three surfaces import from src/shared/types/session.ts, so the type modification must happen before any green-phase dispatch. Explain why parallel dispatch of all three surfaces simultaneously is incorrect — the shared type update would cause import conflicts and the parallel workers would not have consistent type context. State that sequential chaining is required.

2. The Phase 5 dispatch plan in four steps:

Step 1 (green phase): Dispatch backend_developer_worker to modify src/shared/types/session.ts to add the refreshToken field. Write boundary: src/shared/types/session.ts only. This must complete before any surface-specific work.

Step 2 (green phase, chained): Dispatch backend_developer_worker to implement src/auth/session.ts using the updated session type. Write boundary: src/auth/session.ts only. Upstream context: the output from Step 1 (the updated session type file).

Step 3 (green phase, chained): Dispatch frontend_developer_worker to implement src/components/LoginForm.tsx using the updated session type. Write boundary: src/components/LoginForm.tsx only. Upstream context: the output from Step 1.

Step 4 (green phase, chained): Dispatch agentic_engineer_worker to update agents/auth-guard/prompt.md to reference the new refreshToken field. Write boundary: agents/auth-guard/prompt.md only. Upstream context: the output from Step 1.

3. The Phase 4 dispatch plan: red-phase tests for each surface before each green-phase, following the same sequential order.

4. The Phase 1 task tool call to backend_developer_worker for the session type modification (Step 1 green phase). Include the complete dispatch brief with all required fields, the phase set to 'green', the write boundary, the read-only context, and the upstream reference.

Your response should include the dependency analysis, the full sequential dispatch plan, and the first green-phase task tool call.
prompts.rationale: Tests complex sequential prompt-chaining with a shared type file creating a write-boundary seam. Verifies the lead serializes the shared type modification first, then dispatches surface-specific workers sequentially with the updated type as context, rather than parallel-dispatching all three.

prompts.prompt:
You are the builder_lead for this implementation run. The workspace is at /home/zzwf/shared/agent-container/workspace/RL/just-oc/autoresearch/test/builder_lead/run-008-iter-1/. The following artifacts exist:

/workspace/architecture-brief.md — Approved architecture brief for the billing calculator feature (slice-044), specifying that src/billing/calculator.ts must implement two public methods: calculateMonthly(contract: Contract): number and calculateAnnual(contract: Contract): number. The brief notes a code quality concern: the current stub implementation has duplicated calculation logic between the two methods.

/workspace/strategic-slice-brief.md — Approved strategic slice confirming P2 priority and that refactoring to eliminate duplication is in scope.

/workspace/src/billing/calculator.ts — Green-phase implementation complete. Contains calculateMonthly() and calculateAnnual() methods. Both methods currently contain nearly identical logic for computing the base fee, applying the discount, and rounding to two decimal places. The duplication is present but all tests are green.

/workspace/tests/billing/calculator.test.ts — All tests passing. Tests cover: monthly calculation with standard contract, monthly calculation with discount tier, annual calculation with standard contract, annual calculation with discount tier, edge case with zero-value contract, and rounding precision verification.

The prior conversation in this run is as follows:

---
[CEO → builder_lead]: Slice-044 billing calculator. Green phase complete. All tests are passing. The architecture brief flagged duplication between calculateMonthly and calculateAnnual. Proceed to Phase 7 refactor.
---

Your role as builder_lead is to dispatch the Phase 7 refactor. The refactor phase is distinct from the green phase: in green phase the goal is to make failing tests pass; in refactor phase the goal is to improve code structure without changing behavior, while keeping all tests green. The invariant is that all existing tests must remain passing after the refactor.

Produce the following:

1. A written acceptance checklist confirming that all green-phase preconditions are met: all tests in tests/billing/calculator.test.ts are currently passing, the implementation exists in src/billing/calculator.ts, and the architecture brief has been reviewed.

2. A written analysis of the refactoring target: the duplication between calculateMonthly() and calculateAnnual() in src/billing/calculator.ts. Specifically, both methods currently contain the same sequence of: (a) computing the base fee from contract.amount, (b) applying the discount tier based on contract.durationMonths, (c) rounding the result to two decimal places using Math.round(value * 100) / 100. The duplication means that if the rounding behavior changes, it must be changed in two places — a maintenance hazard. State the structural improvement: extract the common calculation into a private method (e.g., computeBaseFee(contract: Contract): number) that both public methods call, eliminating the duplication while preserving the same rounding behavior and discount logic. The refactor must not change the public interface, the return type, or the test behavior — only the internal structure.

3. The Phase 7 dispatch brief for backend_developer_worker: phase = 'refactor', objective = 'Extract duplicated calculation logic in src/billing/calculator.ts into a private method. Both calculateMonthly() and calculateAnnual() must call the new private method. All existing tests must remain passing after refactoring.', write boundary = 'src/billing/calculator.ts only', read-only context = 'architecture brief, tests/billing/calculator.test.ts (invariant oracle)', do-not-touch = 'tests/billing/calculator.test.ts', invariant = 'all tests in tests/billing/calculator.test.ts must pass after refactoring', evidence required = 'test suite output showing all tests still passing, and the refactored src/billing/calculator.ts source showing the private method extraction'.

4. The Phase 8 self-verification plan: after the refactor is complete and tests are green, dispatch a fresh test_engineer_worker instance in audit mode to re-run the test suite and confirm no regressions.

5. The Phase 7 task tool dispatch call to backend_developer_worker with the complete brief described above.

Your response should include the checklist, the duplication analysis, the dispatch brief, and the task tool call.
prompts.rationale: Tests refactor-phase dispatch with explicit invariant set (tests that must stay green). Verifies the lead dispatches a refactor-focused worker with the correct write boundary, the invariant test set, and the structural improvement target.

prompts.prompt:
You are the builder_lead for this implementation run. The workspace is at /home/zzwf/shared/agent-container/workspace/RL/just-oc/autoresearch/test/builder_lead/run-009-iter-1/. The following artifacts exist:

/workspace/architecture-brief.md — Approved architecture brief for the queue processor feature (slice-039), specifying: (1) src/queue/processor.ts — a backend queue consumer that polls a message queue and processes items, emitting 'queue:item-processed' events; (2) src/components/QueueDashboard.tsx — a frontend React component that displays queue depth, processing rate, and recent item IDs; (3) their respective test files. The brief makes four verifiable claims: (a) processor handles 1000 items/second throughput, (b) processor recovers from network disconnects gracefully, (c) QueueDashboard renders real-time updates without page refresh, (d) the system maintains eventual consistency within 500ms.

/workspace/strategic-slice-brief.md — Approved strategic slice confirming P1 priority and that self-verification must cover all four claims from the architecture brief.

/workspace/src/queue/processor.ts — Implementation complete by backend_developer_worker. Green phase passed.
/workspace/src/components/QueueDashboard.tsx — Implementation complete by frontend_developer_worker. Green phase passed.
/workspace/tests/queue/processor.test.ts — All tests passing.
/workspace/tests/components/QueueDashboard.test.tsx — All tests passing.

The prior conversation in this run is as follows:

---
[CEO → builder_lead]: Slice-039 queue processor. All green-phase and refactor-phase work is complete. All tests are green. All three surfaces implemented. Proceed to Phase 8 self-verification. Verify all four claims from the architecture brief against the actual artifacts.
---

Self-verification is a critical quality gate. It must use fresh worker instances — not the same workers that built the artifacts — to provide an independent audit. The goal is to verify that the implementation actually satisfies the architecture brief's claims, not just that tests pass.

Produce the following:

1. A written self-verification plan that covers all four architecture brief claims:

Claim (a) throughput: dispatch a fresh backend_developer_worker in audit mode to run a load test on src/queue/processor.ts and measure actual items/second. The acceptance threshold is 1000 items/second.

Claim (b) graceful recovery: dispatch a fresh backend_developer_worker in audit mode to simulate a network disconnect during processing and verify the processor recovers without data loss or hanging.

Claim (c) real-time updates: dispatch a fresh frontend_developer_worker in audit mode to verify that QueueDashboard uses a reactive data mechanism (WebSocket, polling, or React state) that updates the UI without a page reload.

Claim (d) eventual consistency: dispatch a fresh test_engineer_worker in audit mode to verify that the event propagation from processor to dashboard completes within 500ms.

2. For each self-verification dispatch, specify: the worker archetype, the phase set to 'self-verification', the specific claim being audited, the verification method (how the worker will test the claim), the pass/fail threshold, and the evidence required.

3. The self-verification report compilation plan: after all four fresh-audit dispatches return, synthesize the results into a Build Slice Execution Summary that maps each claim to its verification result, identifies any gaps between the claim and the actual behavior, and states whether the slice passes or fails self-verification.

4. The first Phase 8 task tool dispatch call for the throughput audit using a fresh backend_developer_worker instance with the complete self-verification brief.

Your response should include the self-verification plan, the rationale for using fresh instances, and the first self-verification task tool call.
prompts.rationale: Tests self-verification dispatch using fresh worker instances — not the same workers that built the artifacts. Verifies the lead dispatches fresh test_engineer_worker and fresh developer instances in audit mode, and that the self-verification report covers all claims with oracle-honesty justification.
